title: Trie Insertion Failure Due to Incorrect Child Node Initialization

statement.markdown: Solve the problem and fix the bugs.

examples.input:
5
insert hello
insert world
delete hello
insert help
insert hell

hints.text: Verify the logic of child node initialization in the Trie.
Double-check that the correct node is being updated during insert operations.

editorial.title: Fixing the Trie Insertion Bug
editorial.intuition: The problem lies in the way child nodes are initialized. Instead of using a boolean flag to track if a child node exists, we should simply initialize the child nodes as empty lists.
editorial.bruteForce: The current implementation has a time complexity of O(n^2) due to the recursive function calls and the nested loops. This can be optimized by using a more efficient data structure or algorithm.
editorial.betterSolution: One possible solution is to use a dictionary to store the child nodes, where each key represents a character and the value is another Trie node. This allows us to look up child nodes in constant time.
editorial.optimalSolution: The optimal solution is to use a Trie node class with a dictionary attribute to store the child nodes. This allows us to efficiently insert, delete, and search for words in the Trie.
editorial.proofOfCorrectness: The corrected implementation ensures that child nodes are correctly initialized and updated during insert operations. This prevents the Trie from becoming corrupted.
editorial.dryRun: Suppose we have the following input: insert hello -> insert world -> delete hello -> insert help -> insert hell. The corrected implementation should output: 1 0 1
commonMistakes: Improper node update during insert operations

insert hell
